speaker: interviewer
text: OK, could you read that for me, please?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Today's date is December 12, 1994. We're interviewing Mrs. Irene Traub Weiss. The interview is by Shelley Shore, taking place at Brooklyn, New York, United States, in English.

speaker: interviewee
text: It didn't start yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me a little bit about your life before the Holocaust, about your home life.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I am one of eight children. I was brought up in a very orthodox family-- very nice, between very nice circumstances, harmonious. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Well, we lived in a small town, actually. And everything seemed so peaceful, so nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me the name of the town.

speaker: interviewee
text: The name of the town is Halmi in Hungarian. In Romanian, it's Halmeu. It was Romania in that time. And we lived there. Before, this is Transylvania, actually. The region is Transylvania. It's known about. The vampires came from there. And we lived there for about 15 years. My father had a cabinet-making shop, with a couple of workers-- five, six, maybe. We had a store in the middle of the town, which we-- where we sold our own made furniture. We were quite comfortable, almost well off. We were three girls, five boys. Because the town was small, and because we were also religious, schooling was a special thing, which we couldn't attend. I have only a very few education-- very little education. Uh, 1935, we moved to Satu Mare, which is the famous Satu Mare, where the rabbi-- the famous rabbi-- came. Because the little town didn't give us anymore enough-- the children were bigger. And there wasn't too much to do there. They grew up. So in Satu Mare, one of my sister was a seamstress. One of my sister was a milliner. She had a shop. She sold hats. And the boys went all, first, to the Yeshiva. And then each of-- I don't know if I have to go in about everybody. But that's how we lived.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father actually came from Poland, when he was only 17. And then he got married. He married my mother, of course. And he went to the army. He went to the First World War, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: But in '41, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the Hungarian occupied again the Transylvania. So we became Hungarians. And then the antisemitism started already. They find out, somehow, that my father was born in Poland. And one day, without any preparation, without any thinking about it-- it just, like, came like a shock-- we were transported to Poland, taken out from our home. Luckily, my bigger sisters were married, and my brothers-- some of my brothers. We were home only me and my little brother, David, with my parents. And they took us through the border. They put us down in the middle of a dirt road. The man says, this is our country. This is what our [? Messiah ?] brought to you. Don't dare to come back. You are going to be shot.

speaker: interviewee
text: So somehow-- this is a very long story. I don't want to get into it. It's a separate story. Somehow, our life was in constant danger. Because the Ukrainian killed the people. Most of those people were sent to [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: Many people know about it. And they were all killed by the Germans. That time, Poland was occupied by the Germans. So we just fell into them. We spent a year in Poland. We found my father's sister. And we lived through a terrible, terrible year over there. After a year, we managed to escape. One night, during the [? border, ?] we came back. And then my parents were sent to the jail. It's a very tragic story. We stayed home for two years. And then the Holocaust came. Then we were taken to the ghettos.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, the ghetto is another very sad story. Before we get to the ghetto, can you tell me a little bit, before you got to Poland even, about your childhood. How did you celebrate Jewish holidays, for example?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh my God, of course. Well, I didn't know that-- where is my-- I took it out. It is OK. Sure, the holidays were just beautiful. The Friday nights is the most, most beautiful memory in my whole life.

speaker: interviewer
text: So describe it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh my God. First of all, the Friday we woke up early-- 5 o'clock in the morning. And we had to prepare the bread, which was about 30 lbs of flour-- would you believe it-- to knead, and the challah for Shabbat, and the danishes for Shabbat morning. The whole day was busy, cooking and cleaning the house.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had wooden floors. We had our own home. We had a-- accordingly to those times, we had a large home. We were 10, see? And of course, the whole house has to be cleaned and scrubbed, and everybody was very, very busy. Including ourself-- we scrubbed ourselves, too, once a week. We didn't have a bathroom. We just had a big dish, where we washed ourself. The boys went to the mikveh, to the Jewish ritual bath.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Shabbat came. And we had a long table, with a white table cloth, with a challah where Papa used to sit, covered with a nice, silk, embroidered cloth, the 10 candle lights on the table, which flickered on my sisters' and brothers' faces. [CRYING] Of course, we all listened to Papa, while he chanted the Kiddush. And of course, the Shabbat atmosphere, the Shabbat spirit-- the chicken soup, and the fish, and everything else-- it was quiet, peaceful-- very nice. Friday evening was the nicest from all the holidays. And then the Pesach preparation, of course.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, there wasn't an inch in the house which wasn't cleaned or moved, including the walls-- everything. That's how it was. That-- that-- that was the time, you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: And of course, my sisters later learned their trade. Everybody was doing something. We were a very hard-working family. We were brought up with the spirit that work-- work really makes you somebody. And we all managed, you know, life, yeah, really, thank God.

speaker: interviewee
text: But you see, the Holocaust came. And that was-- that was the Holocaust. What should I tell you? Then we had to leave everything. Luckily, my sister and brother lived in Bucharest. And they lived through the Holocaust, in their house-- apartment, actually.

speaker: interviewee
text: Some other brothers were in the forced labors. And they also lived through. I lost one brother, Moshe, who was the third, after me-- I am a fifth child-- and such a nice little boy-- and a sister, who lived in Budapest. She was married. She wasn't deported, actually. But she was caught by the [NON-ENGLISH] it was called, and just shot in the Danube. They did that with hundreds of Jewish people.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when I came home, I was sure that she was alive. She was such clever girl. Anyway, so the two I lost. And of course, I lost my parents. But my young-- My young years were very, very colorful, very nice, very happy.

speaker: interviewee
text: I am actually a happy type. And I think I saw things differently, maybe. But my-- my-- all my remembering-- you know, my-- if I reminisce, it's-- it's-- the pictures are very nice, and so clear, so clear, those pictures. I'm always sorry I'm not a painter, to paint on our home.

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything-- I remember every picture. I remember my mama and papa, when they were young on the wall. I remember the-- the-- the pillows. We were very handy, all of us. And-- and our house was decorated nicer than almost all the houses I know. This is-- he's not here. What's going on? Do you remember, when you were a child, experiencing any antisemitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I heard my parents talking about that he's an anti-Semite-- anti-Semite-- or the other one is, but no. First of all, the school-- we were almost all Jewish children, because the population was mixed Hungarian, Romanian, and Jewish. And they were mostly Hungarians. And the Hungarians went to parochial schools. Only-- there were only a few Romanian inhabitants, and they went to the public school, where we went. So-- so we were, like, in a-- in a-- in a class, we might be, let's see, 15 Jewish children, and 4 or 5 Romanian children. So Saturday there were no school, because we were Jewish. And-- and we didn't go to school. And they just admitted that. You see, usually the school time, that time, was six days. But in our little town, we didn't. Shabbat, we had a-- a religious man coming. We went to school for one hour. We would call it Sunday-- Sunday school.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he always told us about the weekly Sidra-- yeah? Yeah-- and the story about it. That was-- that was it. School was beautiful. I loved school. I loved school. I loved school. Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: But it ended so fast.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you join any youth organizations? Did they have groups for the children to be in-- Jewish groups?

speaker: interviewee
text: It wasn't. There was no Jewish organization that time, in our town-- not for the children. For bigger ones-- when we moved to Satu Mare, I was already about 16-- 15, 16-- then there was the [NON-ENGLISH], which was young, Jewish girls and women. And there was also the [NON-ENGLISH]. My sisters used to go. Sometimes I went with them to the Oneg Shabbat, they call it, Friday evenings. There was always a speech or something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Satu Mare, was your home life with Shabbat still the same?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, oh, of course. Satu Mare was a famous religious city-- sure, sure. Nothing changed, in that sense-- absolutely nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just more so, because it was bigger?

speaker: interviewee
text: It wasn't more so. We were as religious as we were before, of course. Our religious life didn't change at all, not really. It was a--

speaker: interviewer
text: And the family life-- you were-- not the whole family was there, right? But the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, because one sister was almost married. She was in Bucharest. One brother was in Bucharest. Another brother, Moshe, who came back, was in Papa, in Hungary, in a Yeshiva. Some brothers were at the Yeshivas. And some-- a brother was married-- one of my brothers.

speaker: interviewee
text: So in Satu Mare, we were home only reduced numbers. How old were you when you were in Satu Mare?

speaker: interviewee
text: From 16 till 20. Because I was 20 when we was taken to Poland. And then we came back, of course, from Poland. We were in Poland from '41 until '42-- '41 August. '42 August, about, we came back. They put my parents in the jail, because of that. I managed to escape Satu Mare with my brother. I didn't look Jewish. I was dressed up. And-- I don't know-- by some miracle, I always looked good. I don't know. I was just blessed with that, whatever you call it. Because I had to climb from the borders to our town, which was a six, seven-hour train road. So in that, I was afraid that they are going to check, or to ask us. But we got-- me and my brother, we got home all right. But my parents were caught, and put in jail. And after the jail, they were put in-- put in a some kind of a camp. I don't know how to-- how to tell about that, which they were there for one year, separate. My father-- after Poland, because they came back from Poland. It was illegal, you know. We just--

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you came back to Poland, when you were taken to Poland, can you tell me how that came about? Can you describe to me how you found out that you had to leave and what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: We just got a note from the police, which says that my father has to be in that in that time, in that in that hour, at the police station. We didn't know what it meant. We didn't understand. And we didn't think of anything. But still, the time was already not so good. And we heard rumors that some people are taken to Poland. But because my father came so young, because he lived his life in Hungary, because he was in the Hungarian army, because everything-- so we didn't think of that.

speaker: interviewee
text: But anyway, we asked our-- we asked our lawyer what to do. Well, he says, you know what-- but he didn't know. But don't-- don't let your father go. Go yourself. In those times, you know, we would expect that they would be more polite to a lady than to a bearded man, you see? So I went to the police. Well, this is a long story. I don't want to tell everything, just a little bit from it. And there was a police there. He says, where is your father? I said, he doesn't feel well, as I was taught to tell.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he arrested him. Put him in a cab, bring him over. And this is an order. I couldn't tell one word. I came home. And we went back, with my father, to the same officer, whose face is still in front of me. What he said-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he said so-- that he stood up. And he read a paper, like an order. You-- you are arrested. in the-- in the-- in the name of Hungarian authority-- something like that-- you are arrested, Mr. Gabriel Traub. That was my father. You, Mrs. Traub, go home. Pack. Bring the rest of the family. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I-- I don't know. I can't describe what I felt. I'm wondering I didn't pass out, because that was such a shock. It was so unexpected. And right away, they put two detectives. I was among them-- between them. And they came home. They escorted me. They came home with me. I find my mother's home. And I don't know how I found the words to tell her what happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: I never saw my mother in such a stage. And we had to pack. So we didn't know anything. We didn't have the slightest idea what's going to happen. Where are we going? How long are we going to stay there? What spot? Nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- I was in shock. And I just didn't know how to act. And I didn't know what to pack. It was as much as we can carry in our hands, and about some-- pengos were the money at that time. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So I-- I-- I had a hard time deciding what to pack, because everything is sweet, and everything is close to our heart. And I-- I-- I-- we-- we packed. The neighbors came. And they tried to be nice. And that hurt me so much. And I never had an explanation for myself. Why did it hurt it me so much that the neighbors pitied us? They were nice. They were good. I don't know. They brought some-- because in that time, the food was already rationed, you see? We had the coupons. And of course, there wasn't too much in the house. So they brought certain things, which they thought would be helpful. And we went with the two men. What kind of things did they bring? What kind of things?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like, like, like jam. Like a piece of, let's say, smoked fish, meat, like, dry cookies-- dry things, mostly, which would last. And we didn't have too much room for them, anyway, because we tried to take our winter coat, and our pillow, and a cover, and I don't know what. I remember I took a brief of needles, sewing needles. I don't know how, because I know how to sew. I left that out. And for a needle, I could get a bread in Poland. You know that-- for one needle. I took my sister's silk stockings. I was thinking about it, to take it. And a silk stocking-- they never saw such a thing in their life. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So those times was-- and we couldn't take too much. My little brother was with us. We were four of us. He hide. He was so smart. He was 12 years old. He did hide by a neighbor. And he-- somehow he smelled that something is not right.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the neighbor, maybe with good intention, told him, your mama is looking for you. Go home. If he would stay by the neighbor, he would be saved. So we just closed the doors, and we stepped out. You see, we left the words for the neighbors to telegram, for everybody not to come home. You see, that's how they weren't home, and they just escaped that whole thing. Poland was a very tragic thing-- the most, most, most tragic thing in our life. But we came back, you see. Before-- before they took them to the ghettos, we came back. We came back [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: When you got to Poland, where did you say? In what town? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, sure. First-- first we took us two weeks to get where we wanted to go, actually, because we walked. You see, we walked from one town to the other. And the Jewish communities gave us something to eat, and a shelter in a shul. We'd stroll, we'd-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my father had still a sister there, in Monasterzyska. That's how the town is called. It's Galicia. And we stood there, during the year. That's a terrible story. I don't think I want to get into it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So then, my father had also a brother, where he was born. Nyzhniv-- it's a little, little village.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was there with my little brother. And I was by my father's sister. But they-- they were not good people. We had a very, very tragic, very sad, very terrible time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Meanwhile, in Hungary, where we left, everybody was all right, you see? I wrote to my friends. We got letter. I wrote to my sister, to my-- we had-- we had-- we knew everything about them. And life went on. But here, it was already a tragedy.

speaker: interviewee
text: They took already people from the town where I was. But because I was a foreigner, they didn't touch me, to take me in the [NON-ENGLISH]. I don't know. I had that privilege, I guess. I was already punished, being there. In the [NON-ENGLISH], I was a [NON-ENGLISH]. Here, I was a [NON-ENGLISH]. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Very sad, very sad. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Poland-- I think it was even worse than-- than the Holocaust, because-- because we were-- we were-- we were handled by our own people, you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's not the same thing that a German hits you, or your sister hits you. So that's what it was, actually. That's the reason. You see, I didn't think of it. That's the reason we had such a terrible time. Mama suffered a lot. I didn't understand so well, but through her words, I remember what she used to say. She was in a terrible, terrible situation. I was young. I took it differently.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: 20 Years old. I was a big girl. They call it here a woman, right?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother-- was she worried about the rest of the family, in--

speaker: interviewee
text: Poland?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, because we got letters from them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she--

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually, one of my sisters got married while we were there. She asked my mother what she should do? Of course, we wanted to [INAUDIBLE]. Don't think about it. If you have the right man, get married. She got married. And it was war time, already-- yeah, while we were in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you went back to Hungary, that was in 19--

speaker: interviewer
text: In Poland it was very--

speaker: interviewee
text: Between '41, '42. In Poland, they started already. In Poland, they were-- they were in a terrible, terrible-- when we got to Poland, then-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] then they didn't have, already, their houses. They didn't have their jobs. Everything was restricted. They couldn't go to the market, shopping. They couldn't leave their towns. We had to wear a white band. I had a picture-- I don't know, what happened to it-- with the Magen David on it, in Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: What else? That men supposed to be shaved, no beard. See? All the furs had to be [INAUDIBLE] While we were there, those things happened. Even if you had-- that small fur collar, had to be brought in for the Germans. So Mama made money to replace that collars. She took out a piece from inside somewhere, from the coat, and made a collar. And we got some-- not money, actually. Money wasn't worth anything-- food. Food-- food was important.

speaker: interviewee
text: A piece of bread-- I don't know-- a bundle of flour, two cups of beans, or something like that. Yeah, it was a terrible time over there already, in Poland. Besides our own private sufferings, you see, there were already-- there were very, very strict laws. And there was the Judenrat-- I don't know if you know about that-- who-- who were the right hand of the Nazis, because the Jewish had to ask the Jews for money, jewelry, everything, everything. And then they started the deported-- it was while I was there they gathered, already, the young girls. And they took them to the concentration camp, which I didn't know that time. They didn't know. They thought they-- we are taken to work. As I-- as I mentioned, I was spared because I was a foreigner.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the Judenrat, the Jewish council, did they explain what was happening? Or they just--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, I don't think they knew. Nobody knew. I don't think people knew that they are going, some of them, to their death. Nobody knew that. Nobody knew that. I was in Auschwitz already, when I couldn't believe it, what's happening.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you decided to go back to Hungary--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- It was the last, last time before the tragedy over there. And we found out, over there, that there is a group of people, who are from Hungary, who are going to the borders. And I went to that group, to see their [NON-ENGLISH]. And they told me that they got a so-called [NON-ENGLISH]-- passport-- from the Germans, that they are strangers here. They belong to Hungary. And they can go back. And the Germans gave them [GERMAN]-- that's how it's called in Germany-- to get to the Hungarian borders. And we attached ourselves to that group, and went with them, from town to town. We didn't have too much packages. We were walking. And sometimes the Jewish congregation-- whatever, the Jewish what is it-- they gave us a wagon so we should-- [INAUDIBLE] sometimes. It depended. And it took us-- they gave us four weeks, the Germans, to get there. But it took us much, much longer. And when we were close to the border already, we were told if this is passed, then--then-- then that's a tragedy, that they are going to kill those who-- there were all kind of-- all kind of rumors, you know. But people, in that time in Poland, they were dying for hunger. You could see people on the street, here and there, blown up, blown up. They died just from hunger. But still, [INAUDIBLE] I remember I had a watch, which I got a present. It was a very big thing. We got a lot of things for that watch. And we sold whatever we had-- little things. I don't know. I really-- I don't know how we managed. But we stood in [? Stryi, ?] which is a bigger town, near the border, where we stood for about a couple of weeks, because our paper was over, you see-- expired. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So that's a long and sad story.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we're going to stop now.
